test_cases.input: 45-minute rough cut of a 30-minute single-subject profile documentary. Cut is 15 minutes over and the third act loses energy. Subject is a chef; film follows her preparing for a restaurant opening.
test_cases.notes: Structural note correctly identified that the third act problem was caused by a second-act setup failure, not a third-act content problem. Trim recommendations were specific with estimated time savings.

test_cases.input: 70-minute cut of a 52-minute investigation with four subjects, all roughly equal screen time. Broadcaster feedback: 'we don't know who to care about.' No existing director concerns specified.
test_cases.notes: Notes correctly diagnosed the broadcaster feedback as a protagonist problem — equal screen time was preventing the viewer from investing in any one subject. Structural note recommended a clear hierarchy of subjects rather than parity.

test_cases.input: 88-minute assembly cut (target 75 minutes) of a historical documentary. Multiple archive-heavy sequences. Structural problem suspected in the mid-film transition between two historical periods.
test_cases.notes: Historical period transition problem was correctly identified as a bridge scene problem — the current transition assumed knowledge the viewer hadn't yet been given. Positive observations section was genuinely specific about the strongest archive sequences.
